description: NCERT Class 8 (numbers, scientific notation), Class 11 chemistry & physics (significant figures in measurements, error analysis), and JEE/NEET-style multi-step lab problems.  Covers all 5 sig-fig rules, decimal-place rule for +/-, sig-fig rule for ×/÷, log sig-fig rule, scientific notation conversion, and uncertainty propagation.

questions.question_text: Which measurement is more precise (i.e., has more significant figures)?  100 or 0.0034?
Convert the scientific notation 2.5 × 10^-3 to a plain decimal.  Move the decimal point 3 place(s) to the left, padding with zeros as needed.
Express 566220 in scientific notation rounded to 4 significant figures.
Round 54590.22 to 3 significant figures.  Identify the 3rd sig fig, look at the next digit, and round up if it is 5 or greater.

Compute log(1.7 × 10^-19) and apply the sig-fig rule for logarithms: ONLY the digits after the decimal point in the result count as sig figs (the characteristic — digits before the decimal — depends on the magnitude, not the precision of the argument).
questions.answer_latex: \log(1.7 \times 10^{-19}) = -18.77; \quad \text{rule: } Mantissa has 2 sf → keep 2 digits after the decimal in log. \; \Rightarrow 2 \text{ digits after the decimal.}
questions.answer_plain: -18.77 (2 digits after decimal — log sig-fig rule)

questions.question_text: How many significant figures does 1.3 have?  Apply Rule 4 (trailing zeros after a decimal point ARE significant).
questions.answer_latex: \text{In } 1.3, \text{ all digits including the trailing zeros after the decimal point are significant: } 2 \text{ sig figs.}

questions.question_text: Compute 0.5234 − 0.5230 and discuss the loss of significant figures: when nearly-equal numbers are subtracted, leading digits cancel, leaving only the trailing differences as significant.
questions.answer_latex: 0.5234 - 0.5230 = 0.0004; \; \text{result has only } 1 \text{{ sig fig — many leading digits cancelled.}}

questions.question_text: Compute log(7.320 × 10^-10) and apply the sig-fig rule for logarithms: ONLY the digits after the decimal point in the result count as sig figs (the characteristic — digits before the decimal — depends on the magnitude, not the precision of the argument).
questions.answer_latex: \log(7.320 \times 10^{-10}) = -9.1355; \quad \text{rule: } Mantissa has 4 sf → keep 4 digits after the decimal in log. \; \Rightarrow 4 \text{ digits after the decimal.}
questions.answer_plain: -9.1355 (4 digits after decimal — log sig-fig rule)

questions.answer_latex: \text{In } 1.800, \text{ all digits including the trailing zeros after the decimal point are significant: } 4 \text{ sig figs.}

questions.answer_latex: 0.999 \rightarrow 1.0 \quad (2 \text{{ sf, the trailing zero(s) are required to show precision)}}
